content: The most frequent mistake I see fresh engineers make with iterators is thinking that chaining adapters like map, filter, and flat_map immediately consumes the entire dataset into memory. They write code such as let data = vec! [1, 2, 3]; let result = data.iter().map(|x| x * 2).filter(|x| x > 4).collect::<Vec<_>>(); expecting that the intermediate vectors created by map and filter are small or transient, only to be shocked later when they run out of memory on their dataset. The reality is far more subtle: without a call to collect or for_each, these adapters produce zero-sized lazy values. When you chain iter().map().filter(), each adapter holds no data; it merely returns a new iterator that will yield the next element only when demanded by the caller. This laziness means the entire computation happens at runtime rather than during compilation. The critical insight is understanding that iterators are state machines, not collections. A vector stores all elements in contiguous memory because Vec implements Deref and DerefMut to expose its internal array, but an iterator does not store any data until it is forced to compute more values. When you call collect::<Vec<_>()>, the entire chain of adapters executes immediately, building a full vector in memory before returning. If you instead iterate over this lazy chain with for_each or into_iter(), each element is computed and consumed as it arrives, keeping memory usage minimal even for massive datasets. This distinction matters deeply when processing large files, streaming data, or performing complex transformations where intermediate allocations could crash your application. The common pitfall arises because developers often assume that map creates a new collection containing the mapped results, which is incorrect; map returns an iterator over the mapped elements without storing them. For instance, consider reading from a huge file into a buffer and then filtering lines longer than fifty characters. If you accidentally call collect on this chain before processing further steps, you will allocate a temporary vector holding every line in memory, defeating the purpose of streaming entirely. The correct approach is to keep the pipeline lazy until explicitly forced by collect or for_each, ensuring that only necessary data exists in memory at any given moment. This principle applies universally across Rust's standard library adapters like take_while, skip, and window which all maintain their laziness unless you invoke a method that forces evaluation. By respecting this lazy nature of iterators, you avoid performance pitfalls related to unnecessary allocation and ensure your code scales efficiently with data size.
